mnemonics.title: Specialization vs Generalization Direction
mnemonics.content:
S.T.A.R.T - Specialization Starts Top-down, Abstracts Reduced Types
G.O.F.R.O.M - Generalization Goes From Objects to More general

Remember: S = Super to Sub (top-down), G = Group up (bottom-up)

mnemonics.title: Constraint Types Memory
mnemonics.content:
T.P.A.R.T.I.A.L - Total Participation All Required, Partial Is Allowed Less

D.O. - Disjoint Only one, Overlapping - can Overlap multiple

acronyms.usage: Represents the hierarchical relationship between superclass and subclasses in ER diagrams
When a subclass is defined as union of multiple superclasses

visualTips.visualization: Draw an inverted triangle with 'ISA' inside. Place superclass above, connected to triangle base. Draw lines from triangle sides to each subclass below. Double line from superclass to triangle = total participation.

Use circle notation: write 'd' inside small circle on ISA line for disjoint (like division). Write 'o' for overlapping (like sharing/overflow). Disjoint = separate non-overlapping circles. Overlapping = Venn diagram with intersection.
Think of a tree: Specialization goes from trunk (superclass) to branches (subclasses) - downward. Generalization goes from leaves (subclasses) up to trunk (superclass) - upward movement.

analogies.analogy: Like a university faculty being specialized into Professor, Associate Professor, Assistant Professor - starting from general 'Faculty' to specific roles.
Like different types of bank accounts (Savings, Current, Fixed Deposit) being generalized into a common 'Bank Account' entity with shared attributes.
Like children inheriting eye color or height from parents - they get these traits automatically without being specifically assigned.
Like being either a student OR a teacher at a school - you can't be both simultaneously (in traditional sense).
Like someone who is both a Professor and a Researcher - one person can belong to multiple categories at the same time.
